coredata.dc:title: Cycle-efficient modeling for degradation staging and early life prediction of lithium batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Can
Wang, Renjie
Li, Jianming
Li, Zhuangzhuang
Yu, Quanqing
coredata.dc:description: An effective and time-saving early life prediction model is crucial for rapid battery assessment. However, existing models face a dilemma: they either rely heavily on extensive historical data or provide limited predictive insights into battery degradation. To address this, this study proposes a cycle-efficient battery life assessment framework integrating data-driven and empirical models. The framework consists of two components: degradation stage detection relying solely on data from one cycle and early life prediction using five-cycle data. The early life prediction model is capable of achieving joint prediction of the battery's remaining useful life and the cycle to knee point. Experimental results demonstrate that the degradation staging model achieves an accuracy of 0.977,6 for lithium iron phosphate batteries. Meanwhile, the early life prediction model yields mean absolute percentage errors of 10.5% for remaining useful life and 12.8% for the cycle to knee predictions. The model's accuracy and generalizability have been validated across diverse battery types, health states, and operating conditions. This proposed framework exhibits excellent generalizability capability under all evaluated scenarios, establishing a robust foundation for rapid battery design assessment and retirement decisions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Degradation stage detection
Early life prediction
Knee point
Remaining useful life

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of early life prediction model
Transfer learning across highly specific conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The capacity degradation trajectory of two datasets with different battery materials. (a) Dataset-LFP, (b) Dataset-NCA.
The early life prediction model proposed in this work. (a) Identification of model features and prediction labels. (b) Early life prediction model.

Performance of the battery degradation staging model. (a) Test accuracy of ten random batches. (b) The misdiagnosed cycles of the model in batch 7. (c) The AEs between the cycles of model misdiagnosis and the knee points on Test Task 1. (d) The effect of different test set sizes on model accuracy. (e) The misdiagnosed cycles of the model on Test Task 2. (f) The AEs between the cycles of model misdiagnosis and the knee points on Test Task 2. (g) The detection accuracy on Test Task 2.

Performance of the proposed early life prediction model in Dataset-LFP. (a) and (b) The true and prediction value of CTK and RUL. (c) The AEs of CTK and RUL. (d) and (e) The true and prediction value of QRk and QRe. (f) The AEs of QRk and QRe. (g) and (h) The test metrics of CTK, RUL, QRk and QRe in the repeated 10 batches.

Results of the three-parameter empirical model for predicting battery degradation trajectory. (a)–(h) The prediction and true trajectory of the battery, (a) #19, (a) #19, (b) #28, (c) #32, (d) #33, (e) #103, (f) #1024, (h) #131. (i) Pearson correlation coefficient of the prediction and true trajectory.
Proposed model performance of five different cases. (a) CTK prediction, (b) RUL prediction, (c) QRk prediction, (d) QRe prediction.

Performance of the proposed model in five different batches. (a) and (b) The prediction and true values of CTK and RUL in Batch 1. (c) MAPE of CTK and RUL in five batches. (d) and (e) The prediction and true values of QRk and QRe in Batch 1. (f) MAPE of QRk and QRe in five batches.
Principal components of the last LSTM layer output. (a) Case 1, (b) Case 2, (c) Case 3, (d) Case 4, (e) Case 5.

Performance of the proposed early life prediction model for NCA batteries. (a) and (b) The true and prediction value of CTK and RUL. (c) The AEs of CTK and RUL. (d) and (e) The true and prediction value of QRk and QRe. (f) The AEs of QRk and QRe. (g) and (h) The test metrics of CTK, RUL, QRk and QRe in the repeated 10 batches.
Results of the three-parameter empirical model for predicting NCA battery degradation trajectory. (a) Cell 5, (b) Cell 12, (c) Cell 17, (d) Cell 21, and (e) Cell 22.
The detailed description of the test task and batteries in the following section.

row.entry: The accuracy validation of the battery degradation staging model.
The generalization validation across different battery materials of the degradation staging model.
The accuracy validation of the battery early life prediction model.
The few-shot transfer learning ability validation of the battery early life prediction model for the significantly different operating conditions.
The generalization validation across different battery materials of the battery early life prediction model.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of accuracy between the proposed degradation staging model and existing models.

row.entry: A sequence of 50 discrete points for Q, V, and IC extracted from one-cycle data.
A sequence of 50 discrete points for Q, V, and IC extracted from one-cycle data.
Discrete sequences of voltage, current, and temperature for three cycle data.
Six model parameters extracted from the relaxation voltage curve based on the 2RC equivalent circuit model and the relaxation temperature.
The training set include all the operating conditions of the test set.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of accuracy between the proposed early life prediction model and existing models.

row.entry: A sequence of 50 discrete points for Q, V, and IC extracted from five cycle data.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fitting parameters of the empirical model.
Five different cases for applying the proposed model in different data domain.

row.entry: Fine-tune the parameters of the model's feature extraction module while freezing prediction module.
Fine-tune the parameters of the model's prediction module while freezing feature extraction module.
Adjust the training and testing set ratio in the target domain without performing transfer learning.
Train and test the model with the dataset same as Case 1, using only 3 batteries for training.
Test the model with applying the source domain parameters to the target domain.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: Battery 4.0 published in Green Energy and Intelligent Transportation.

head.ce:title: Cycle-efficient modeling for degradation staging and early life prediction of lithium batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechatronics Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechatronics Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automotive Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Weihai, Shandong 264209, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The University of Hong Kong, Pokfulam Road, Hong Kong, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Mechatronics Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An effective and time-saving early life prediction model is crucial for rapid battery assessment. However, existing models face a dilemma: they either rely heavily on extensive historical data or provide limited predictive insights into battery degradation. To address this, this study proposes a cycle-efficient battery life assessment framework integrating data-driven and empirical models. The framework consists of two components: degradation stage detection relying solely on data from one cycle and early life prediction using five-cycle data. The early life prediction model is capable of achieving joint prediction of the battery's remaining useful life and the cycle to knee point. Experimental results demonstrate that the degradation staging model achieves an accuracy of 0.977,6 for lithium iron phosphate batteries. Meanwhile, the early life prediction model yields mean absolute percentage errors of 10.5% for remaining useful life and 12.8% for the cycle to knee predictions. The model's accuracy and generalizability have been validated across diverse battery types, health states, and operating conditions. This proposed framework exhibits excellent generalizability capability under all evaluated scenarios, establishing a robust foundation for rapid battery design assessment and retirement decisions.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used in electric vehicles and energy storage systems due to their advantages such as long lifespan and high specific power [1–3]. During the battery development and service stages, it is crucial to quickly understand how the battery will degrade in the future [4,5]. However, long-term cycling tests can take several years, which is difficult to meet the demand. It is necessary to develop an efficient and time-saving early life prediction model of LIBs for meeting the quick battery assessment [6,7].

Battery life prediction methods, including degradation trajectory, knee point, remaining useful life (RUL), and hybrid predictions [8,9], vary in comprehensiveness and complexity. Among these, degradation trajectory offers the most detailed insight into battery aging, while most alternative indicators provide only scalar estimates of lifespan. As a result, considerable research efforts have been devoted to the prediction of battery degradation trajectories. Li et al. [10] proposed a deep learning model for one-shot battery degradation trajectory prediction, eliminating the need for iterative processes. However, their approach requires a minimum of 100 cycles of data. Lu et al. [11] tackle the challenge of trajectory prediction under uncertain future operating conditions by integrating future current profiles and early capacity-voltage data. Nevertheless, their approach imposes relatively stringent requirements on the model inputs. Lin et al. [12] successfully predicted battery life trajectories by augmenting the dataset; however, their model still necessitates data from at least 100 cycles. Han et al. [13] achieved capacity trajectory prediction by extracting two features correlated with capacity based on constant-current discharge voltage curves. However, they validated the model using data from the initial cycle up to 95% state of health (SOH) as feature inputs. In general, while degradation trajectory prediction provides the most comprehensive insights, it also entails the highest level of effort. Most existing methods require at least 100 cycles of data, making them time-consuming and incompatible with the urgent needs of battery design and retirement assessment.

Since the high-data-demand issue of degradation trajectory prediction is difficult to overcome, some research scholars shifted focus to predicting battery knee points and RUL as alternative measures of battery lifetime [14]. Zhao et al. [15]and Ma et al. [16] extracted features from data spanning 30 and 40 cycles, respectively, and developed deep learning models to estimate RUL. Notably, Ma et al. [17] discovered that by extracting incremental capacity (IC) features from a single charging curve and constructing a deep neural network, RUL prediction could be achieved with a root mean square error (RMSE) of only 11.42 cycles. Similarly, Wang et al. [18] and Li et al. [19] employed a Transformer model and a hierarchical Bayesian model, respectively, to predict RUL. In general, RUL prediction is relatively straightforward and can be achieved with less cycling data, while RUL provides limited insights into the overall degradation behavior. In addition, researchers have also paid attention to battery knee points, which represent the point where the degradation rate undergoes a significant change [20,21]. The knee point can divide the battery capacity trajectory into two degradation stages: a slow degradation stage and a rapid degradation stage. Fermín-Cueto et al. [22] and You et al. [23] proposed two methods for identifying nonlinear knee points, respectively, and successfully achieved knee point prediction. Sohn et al. [24] developed a battery knee-onset classification model and a knee point prediction model, achieving a mean absolute error (MAE) of 14 cycles for knee point prediction. Ke et al. [25] established 70 features from data of 30 cycles through 14 functions and 5 transformations, which were then used in a convolutional neural network (CNN) to achieve knee point prediction. Lee et al. [26] also achieved early prediction of battery knee points by utilizing CNN and recurrent neural network (RNN) based on 60 cycles of data. However, predicting knee points alone also has limitations as it contains relatively less degradation information.

To overcome the limited degradation insights provided by RUL and knee point predictions, researchers identified the end-of-life (EOL) and knee points as critical markers in battery degradation trajectories. By developing a joint model to predict RUL and cycle to knee (CTK) point, an approximate battery degradation trajectory can be reconstructed through function fitting. This approach offers a promising solution for the rapid and targeted assessment of battery lifespan. Haris et al. [27] constructed a CNN network using data from the first cycle to predict the knee point and EOL point, subsequently fitting the degradation trajectory from the starting point. Strange et al. [28] and Ibraheem et al. [29] also employed a comparable approach to predict battery capacity and internal resistance trajectories. Fan et al. [30] utilized discrete relaxation voltage as features to construct a joint prediction framework for battery RUL and knee point, achieving prediction MAEs all below 26 cycles. However, the aforementioned methods still rely on a relatively large number of cycling data on one hand, and on the other hand, the proposed models have not been validated under various conditions such as the full lifecycle of batteries or different battery material systems. Consequently, the limitations of these models remain unclear.

In summary, direct prediction of battery degradation trajectories still requires substantial historical data. While the standalone prediction of RUL or knee point partially mitigates this issue, it sacrifices degradation information completeness. A potential solution to this dilemma lies in first performing joint prediction of battery RUL and knee point using limited historical data, then determining the relative positions of the battery knee point and EOL point from the current state point, and finally reconstructing the battery degradation trajectory using empirical models. This approach may open up new avenues for resolving the conflict between the need for extensive historical data and the limitations of degradation information. However, further in-depth research is still needed to explore the generalization capabilities of related methods across the full lifecycle of batteries, different material systems, and various feature conditions. In response, this study proposes a rapid battery life assessment framework, which includes battery degradation staging model and early life prediction model. This framework aims to address the aforementioned challenges and provide an effective solution for the assessment of battery design and retirement. The contributions of this study are as follows:
a)
Degradation staging model: Enables the discrimination of slow or rapid degradation behaviors throughout the entire battery lifecycle of different material systems using a single cycle of data.
b)
Early life prediction model: Simultaneously forecasts RUL, CTK, capacity at the EOL point, and capacity at the knee point of batteries, paired with empirical trajectory reconstruction.
c)
Generalizable framework: The proposed early life prediction model requires only 5 cycles of battery data and demonstrates excellent generalizability across various health stages, battery material systems, and highly specific operating conditions.

The remaining sections of this study are organized as follows: Section 2 introduces the publicly available datasets used for model validation. Section 3 provides a detailed explanation of the proposed degradation staging model and early life prediction model. Section 4 discusses the performance and generalizability of the models under highly specific conditions. Section 5 concludes the study.

In this research, two datasets from distinct battery materials are employed to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The datasets are sourced from Severson et al. [31] and Zhu et al. [32], respectively. The first dataset utilized LFP/graphite batteries, which underwent aging tests in three different batches. The dataset encompasses multiple aging conditions, and the batteries within different batches exhibited varying levels of calendar aging. Even under the same aging conditions, significant trajectory variability was observed among the batteries, thus effectively validating the robustness of the proposed method. It should be noted that due to abnormal test data or relatively small life degradation in some batteries, a screening process was conducted, and ultimately 92 batteries were selected for this study. These batteries were numbered, as detailed in Supplementary Table S1. Furthermore, the battery lifetimes are pre-classified with reference to Ref. [12], in order to verify the performance of the model on few samples, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). The second dataset employed NCA/graphite batteries to validate the generalization ability of the model across different battery materials, as shown in Fig. 1(b). A detailed description of the dataset can be found in Ref. [32]. Similarly, the batteries are numbered, as detailed in Supplementary Table S2. The two datasets will be referred to as Dataset-LFP and Dataset-NCA in subsequent discussions, respectively.

The battery degradation staging model and early life prediction model are presented in this section, as shown in Figs. 2 and 3
, respectively. The battery degradation staging model can accurately determine whether a battery has entered the rapid degradation stage using only one-cycle data. On the other hand, the early life prediction model requires only five-cycle data to quickly identify the knee point and EOL point. Based on these points, an empirical model can then be used to construct the battery degradation trajectory.

The battery degradation staging model primarily consists of three modules: feature engineering, degradation stage labeling, and classification model, as shown in Fig. 2. Firstly, this study extracts battery voltage, capacity, and IC sequences from one-cycle data, and discretizes them into 50 points to ensure a uniform input dimension for the model. Considering that discretizing the data into 50 points is sufficient and not the focus of this study, so there is no further discussion on this matter. Besides, it should be noted that the proposed model does not rely on fixed charging or discharging features. In subsequent sections, the applicability of the model on both charging and discharging features are verified. Secondly, the battery knee point is identified by the maximum distance method, as shown in Fig. 3(a). The distance from each point on the battery degradation trajectory to the line connecting the starting point of battery degradation and the EOL point is calculated, and the point with the maximum distance is identified as the knee point [33]. Then, one-hot encoding is used to represent the battery degradation stages, where the battery is considered to be in a slow degradation stage before reaching the knee point and in a rapid degradation stage after it. These stages are represented as Stage 1 and Stage 2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2. Finally, a classification model is constructed to detect the degradation stage, which includes one-dimensional convolutional layers, max pooling layers and fully connected layers. As shown in Fig. 2, the structure and feature dimensions of the model are presented. The effect of model structure parameters on the accuracy of degradation stage detection is not the focus of this work. Therefore, this discussion is not presented in subsequent sections.

In order to reduce the test time required for battery life prediction, the combination of data-driven model and empirical model is proposed in this work. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the battery capacity degradation trajectory can be controlled by three points: the starting point, the knee point, and the EOL point. In this study, the abscissa of each point represents the cycle number, and the ordinate corresponds to the capacity. Using the coordinates of these three points, a three-parameter empirical model can be used to redraw the battery degradation trajectory. Vermeer et al. [34] pointed out that the relationship between the battery capacity and the cycle number can be described by the power-law, and the power-law exponent is considered to be related to the growth of the battery's solid electrolyte interface. Therefore, in this study, an empirical model was constructed based on the power function relationship, as shown in Eq. (1). In addition, four prediction labels are adopted in this study, as shown in Eqs. (2)–(5). With these four prediction labels and the known starting point coordinates, the coordinates of the knee point and EOL point can be determined.
(1)
Q = a C y
c b
+ c
(2)
Q
R k
=
Q s
Q k
(3)
Q
R e
=
Q s
Q e
(4)
CTK =
C k
−
C s
(5)
RUL =
C e
−
C s
where, Cyc represent the cycle number. a, b, c are the fitting parameters. Q s, Q k and Q e represent the capacity of starting point, knee point and EOL point, respectively. QR k and QR e represent the ratios of the starting point capacity to the knee point capacity and the EOL point capacity, respectively. C s, C k and C e represent the cycle number of starting point, knee point and EOL point, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3(b), the proposed early life prediction model is comprised of a feature extraction module and a prediction module with fully-connected neural (FCN) network. Attempting to reduce the demand for cycle data, the feature extraction module with the graph attention network (GAT) as core, CNN and long short-term memory (LSTM) network as auxiliaries is specially designed. This design is inspired by the information transfer mechanism of the graph nodes. By equating each battery cycle to a graph node, the information among different cycles can be transferred to each other. However, high-dimensional features cannot be directly transmitted to the graph nodes. Therefore, a CNN is first used to perform pre-fusion processing on the features. Finally, the graph nodes are restored to a time series, and the LSTM is used to complete the feature extraction. The designed feature extraction module can not only fuse different features in each cycle, such as the IC, capacity (Q), and voltage (V) sequences, but also enable information interaction among different cycles.

A sliding window approach is used to construct the training and testing datasets. The sliding window size is set 5 and the step size is set 1. The five-cycle data prior to the prediction starting point are selected, and the Q, V, and IC features are extracted from each cycle, which are then discretized into 50 points. The CNN performs convolutions among three features, forming one-dimensional features. Subsequently, the GAT is utilized to facilitate information transfer among features across 5 cycles. Following this, the graph nodes are converted back into a time series and fed into the LSTM, where the maximum discharge capacity feature is also incorporated here. The inclusion of this feature at this moment is due to the dimension difference among the maximum discharge capacity, Q, V, and IC features. Finally, the last output of the LSTM is passed to two FCN prediction modules, which predict the knee point and EOL point coordinates respectively. The purpose of employing two prediction modules is to enable the EOL point prediction module to operate independently when the knee point does not exist for the battery. Finally, the battery capacity degradation trajectory is reconstructed through the three-parameter empirical formula, as shown in Eq. (1). As shown in Table S3, the specific structural parameters of the model are provided.

In this section, the accuracy of the battery degradation staging model and the early life prediction model are validated, as well as their generalization across different battery materials. Additionally, the few-shot transfer learning ability, when applied to scenarios involving the same battery materials but under highly specific operating conditions, is also discussed. The test tasks, feature conditions and test battery numbers are shown in Table 1 . It should be noted that the total number of batteries used in Test Task 2 and Test Task 5 differs because some batteries in the dataset do not have knee points and cannot be used for Test Task 5. The training and testing batteries are randomly divided. Multiple random validation results are also presented later. To verify that the model has fewer restrictions on features, different feature extraction conditions and voltage ranges are adopted for different batteries. For Dataset-LFP, segments between 2.5 and 3.5 ​V are selected from the discharge voltage curve, while for Dataset-NCA, segments between 3.5 and 4 ​V are selected from the charge voltage curve. It should be noted that in this study, the voltage range was selected with the inclusion of the IC peak as criterion, because the features within this range exhibit a more significant changing trend with degradation, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S1. The specific values of the upper and lower limits of the voltage range are considered less important, so there was no excessive discussion about them.

Test Task 1. The classification accuracy is employed to evaluate the performance of the battery degradation staging model, which is defined as the ratio of correctly classified sample number to the total sample number. To eliminate performance concerns arising from the division of the training and testing sets, 10 random batches of the dataset division and model training and testing are performed. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the test results from the 10 batches indicate that the model accuracy is not affected by the dataset division, and the accuracy is amazing, ranging from a minimum of 0.94 to a maximum of 0.98. This also demonstrates the excellent performance of the proposed detection model for LFP batteries. Subsequently, the test results of Batch 7 are further observed, as shown in Fig. 4(b). It can be seen that most of the misdiagnoses by the model occur near the knee points, which is understandable since the transition between degradation stages is not instantaneous and can be considered as occurring within a reasonable range rather than at a precise knee point. Additionally, Cell 66 has a higher number of misdiagnoses, which is due to the specific characteristics of the battery. This issue can be addressed by increasing the corresponding training samples. Meanwhile, the absolute error (AE) between the misdiagnosed cycles and the knee points was calculated, as shown in Fig. 4(c). Except for Cell 66 and a few individual points, the misdiagnosis of other cells is concentrated near the knee points, with mainly no more than 50 cycles. To further verify the robustness of the model, tests are conducted using five different proportions of testing sets and training sets. As shown in Fig. 4(d), an increase in the number of testing sets results in not significant degree of decrease in model accuracy. When the number of testing sets reaches 50, the test accuracy is 0.95, which is only a 2.8% decrease compared to when the number of testing sets is 10.

Test Task 2. To further verify the proposed model performance, another model training and testing using the Q, V and IC features extracted from the charging curves of NCA batteries are conducted, as shown in Table 1. Compared to Test Task 1, Test Task 2 changed the feature extraction conditions and the battery type, allowing for the verification of the model's generalization across different battery types and feature types. As shown in Fig. 4(e) and (f), the model still performs excellently on Test Task 2, with most misdiagnoses occurring near the knee points of the batteries. The maximum distance between the misdiagnosed cycles and the knee points does not exceed 15 cycles, and the detection accuracy reaches 0.967,3. It demonstrates that the proposed degradation staging model is well generalized across different battery types and feature conditions. The development of battery degradation staging model holds significant application value for rapid battery assessment.

To further demonstrate the accuracy of the proposed degradation staging model, the accuracy of existing models using the same dataset is compared, as shown in Table 2 . Since the accuracy of the model is affected by the training and testing set size, adjustments are made in this section to retrain and retest the proposed model. Additionally, it should be noted that although different studies use the same dataset, due to differences in criteria for screening abnormal samples, the total number of batteries in the dataset may vary slightly. However, the proportion of the training set to the testing set remains similar. Therefore, this allows for a comparison of model performance between different studies. As shown in Table 2, for Dataset-LFP, the proposed model achieves a detection accuracy of 0.963 when the ratio of testing set to training set samples is 40:52. In contrast, the model proposed by Sohn et al. [24] has an accuracy of only 0.91 when the ratio of testing set to training set samples is 10:104. Additionally, although the model proposed by Zhao et al. [35] has a relatively high accuracy of 0.927,5, it is still lower than the model proposed in this study. For Dataset-NCA, the proposed model achieves a detection accuracy of 0.875,3 when the ratio of the testing set to the training set is 20:26. Compared to previous results, this accuracy is slightly lower. This is mainly due to the completely random division of the dataset, and the NCA battery dataset contains multiple different operating conditions, such as 25, 35, and 45 ​°C. When the distribution of the training set and test set differs significantly, the detection accuracy of the model will decrease, and this issue can be addressed by increasing the number of training set samples. For example, Zhao et al. [35] artificially divided the training set and testing set samples to ensure that the battery operating conditions in the training set encompass all those in the testing set, thereby achieving a higher model detection accuracy, averaging 0.910,3. However, the prerequisite for such a dataset division is difficult to achieve in practical processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of early life prediction model
ce:section.ce:para: Through the battery degradation staging model, accurate discrimination of the battery degradation stage can be achieved using only one-cycle data. For batteries in the slow degradation stage (i.e., before the knee point), it is extremely necessary to understand their future degradation trajectory. Therefore, this section developed a battery life prediction model that requires only five-cycle data, which is insignificant for the average lifespan of current commercial batteries. For batteries in the rapid degradation stage, their future number of degradation cycles is limited, and the necessity for predicting their degradation trajectory is relatively weak. Therefore, it is not the focus of this study and will not be discussed.

Test Task 3. Firstly, the accuracy of the proposed model in predicting four labels: CTK, RUL, QRk, and QRe is verified, as shown in Fig. 5 (a)–(f). The results demonstrate that the model exhibits high accuracy in predicting CTK and RUL, with most AEs falling within 50 cycles, which is relatively insignificant compared to the average lifespan of commercial batteries. Furthermore, the model's accuracy in predicting QRk and QRe is unexpectedly high, with the maximum AE not exceeding 0.01. It is worth noting that the prediction accuracy for Cell 131 is relatively poor. This is likely due to the significant variability in operating conditions for Cell 131, particularly as its lifespan approaches Class 3, as mentioned in Section 2. However, as shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), it is found that the larger prediction AEs for Cell 131 mainly occur at the beginning of its life cycle. As the battery aging information becomes more prominent, the prediction accuracy tends to converge, as illustrated more clearly in Fig. 6 (h). To further demonstrate the performance of the proposed model, ten batches of different training and testing set partitions are conducted. The model is trained and tested accordingly. As shown in Fig. 5(g) and (h), the ten batches of model test results exhibit well consistency. The maximum MAPEs for the predictions of CTK and RUL are 12.8% and 10.5%, respectively, while the maximum MAPEs for the predictions of QRk and QRe are 0.5% and 0.3%, respectively. It should also be noted that there are individual batches with slightly poorer test accuracy, which may be related to the fact that this study does not deliberately ensure that the battery conditions in the training set fully cover those in the testing set. Increasing the number of dataset samples is expected to be a potential solution to this issue.

To further validate the model's performance, the study compares the accuracy of the proposed early life prediction model with existing models, as shown in Table 3 . Severson et al. [31] first proposed using ΔQ 100-10 (V) as features to achieve RUL prediction. The model achieved a MAPE of 13% and 10.1% with and without outlier battery data, respectively. Subsequently, Zhang et al. [36] used similar features to predict CTK and RUL, achieving MAPEs of 13.48% and 12.37% based on the gaussian process regression (GPR) model, and 12.66% and 11.86% based on the partial least squares regression (PLSR) model. Safavi et al. [37] also used statistical features of ΔQ 100-10 (V) to predict RUL, achieving a MAPE of 11.3%. In comparison, the model proposed in this study demonstrates higher accuracy, with MAPEs of 12.8% and 10.5% for CTK and RUL predictions, respectively. Compared with Zhang et al. [36], the proposed model achieves at least an 11.5% improvement in RUL prediction accuracy. Furthermore, the aforementioned models require at least 100 test cycles, whereas the proposed model only needs data from 5 cycles, significantly reducing the testing time.

Through accurate predictions of CTK, RUL, QRk and QRe, the relative positions of the battery's knee point, EOL point, and the prediction starting point can be determined, thereby establishing the coordinates of these three points. It then, the three unknown parameters in Eq. (1) presented in Section 3.2 can be identified by the least squares method, allowing for the derivation of a three-parameter empirical model for the subsequent degradation trajectory of the battery. This study reconstructed the battery capacity trajectory for the test set of Batch 10, with the prediction starting point set at the fifth cycle when the battery began to degrade. It is generally acknowledged that the initial cycle data contain little aging information, which further demonstrates the superiority of the proposed model. The parameters of the empirical model are detailed in Table 4 .

As shown in Fig. 6, the proposed three-parameter empirical model can well fit the battery's capacity degradation trajectory. Although there are some differences compared to the measured trajectory, this is acceptable for rapid battery assessment in the real world. This is because, compared to models that mainly rely on 100 cycles of data, the proposed model only requires data from 5 cycles, reducing the required number of test cycles by 95%. This reduction in requirements has significant implications for user experience, power consumption, and other aspects. In addition, it is also noticed that the test accuracy of Cell 131 is slightly poorer. As mentioned earlier, this may be due to the larger differences in operating conditions for Cell 131. However, the study also observed that as the battery continues to degrade, the battery aging information contained in the data from 5 cycles will also increase, thereby improving the accuracy of the model. As shown in Fig. 6(h), when the prediction starting point reaches 100th cycle, the fitting accuracy of the battery capacity trajectory also improves. The fitting accuracy of the capacity trajectory was further quantified using the Pearson correlation coefficient, as shown in Fig. 6(i). Except for Cell 131, the similarity of the capacity trajectories for all tested batteries reached 0.98.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfer learning across highly specific conditions
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the introduction, it is difficult to achieve model generalization across highly specific operating conditions. One solution is to rely on the comprehensiveness of the operating conditions covered by the training set, and the other is to rely on transfer learning. In comparison, the former requires a significant amount of work and may not be highly feasible in the real world, as different cities and charging protocols can produce a wide variety of operating conditions that are difficult to fully cover. Relatively speaking, transfer learning has higher feasibility. Therefore, this study conducts the next test task to verify the model's few-shot transfer learning ability, as shown in Table 5 .

Test Task 4. Five cases are designed in this test task for comparative analysis on issues such as whether to transfer and which modules of the model transfer strategy to use. Case 1 fine-tunes the parameters of the model's feature extraction module while freezing the parameters of the prediction module. Incidentally, detailed descriptions of the feature extraction module and prediction module can be found in Section 3.2. Case 2 freezes the parameters of the feature extraction module and fine-tunes the parameters of the prediction module. Case 3 does not perform transfer learning, instead adjusting the split ratio of the training and testing sets in the target domain for training and testing. Case 4 also does not perform transfer learning and does not adjust the training and testing set ratio in the target domain, using only 3 batteries for training as in Cases 1 and 2. Case 5 does not perform transfer learning and directly applies the source domain model parameters to the target domain for testing. Through the comparative analysis of these five cases, the study will clearly demonstrate the performance of the proposed model, as outlined below. As shown in Fig. 7 , the MAE, RMSE, and MAPE of five cases are compared. The results indicate that Cases 1 and 3 exhibit smaller errors, particularly in predicting the two key features of CTK and RUL, as shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b). For CTK prediction, both Case 1 and Case 3 have a prediction MAPE of 13%, which matches the current performance of related research as mentioned earlier. For RUL prediction, Case 1 and Case 3 have prediction MAPEs of 8% and 6% respectively, demonstrating high accuracy. The slightly lower prediction accuracy for CTK may be attributed to the difficulty in precisely determining the knee point of the batteries, as mentioned in Section 4.1. Besides, as shown in Fig. 7(c) and (d), in terms of QRe prediction, Cases 1 and 3 also exhibit high prediction accuracy, with MAPEs of 0.5% and 0.3% respectively. For QRk prediction, although the prediction accuracy of Case 1 is not the closest to Case 3, its MAPE is also relatively low, at 0.7%. By comparing the accuracy of Case 1 and Case 3, it can be observed that fine-tuning the parameters of the model's feature extraction module with only 3 training batteries in the target domain can achieve similar accuracy to that obtained by re-partitioning the target domain dataset for full model training and testing. This demonstrates the effectiveness of fine-tuning the feature extraction module in the model and verifies the proposed model's ability to perform few-shot transfer learning. Additionally, as shown in Case 5, the results of directly applying the source domain model parameters to test batteries in the target domain are quite disappointing. The prediction MAPEs for CTK and RUL are 51% and 45% respectively. By fine-tuning the parameters of the prediction module of the proposed model, the prediction errors can be reduced to some extent, but the prediction MAPEs for CTK and RUL still remain unacceptable at 34% and 18% respectively. Retraining the model with only three batteries from the target domain can significantly reduce the prediction errors, resulting in prediction MAPEs of 18% and 11% for CTK and RUL respectively. Yet, this is not the optimal solution as training the model with a small number of batteries can easily lead to overfitting, resulting in poor generalization performance. Overall, Case 1 demonstrates well prediction accuracy, achieving the desired test accuracy with only three batteries used for fine-tuning the parameters of the proposed model.

To further demonstrate the performance of the proposed model and transfer strategy (Case 1), five batches of different training and testing datasets were randomly generated. Subsequently, the model was fine-tuned and tested separately for each batch. As shown in Fig. 8 , the maximum prediction MAPE for battery CTK and RUL across the five batches were 15.6% and 10.1%, respectively, while the maximum prediction MAPE for QRk and QRe were 0.77% and 0.64%, respectively. It can be seen that the proposed model exhibits good accuracy, enabling transfer learning across different domains with only a limited number of batteries.

To elucidate the reasons for the differences in model accuracy between Cases 1–5, the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is conducted on the output h t of the last LSTM layer, as shown in Fig. 3. PCA is a method for reducing the dimension of large datasets while retaining the principal components of the original information. For a comprehensive introduction to related theories, please refer to Ref. [38]. As shown in Fig. 9 (a) and (c), the principal components of Case 1 and Case 3 are relatively similar, especially PC1 and PC2. This also explains why the feature extraction module can learn the feature distribution of the batteries in the target domain by fine-tuning the model parameters with only three training batteries, resulting in the model accuracy of Case 1 being comparable to that of Case 3. Although the projections of PC1 and PC3 suggest that the distributions of Case 1 and Case 3 are not completely similar, it can be observed that their evolutionary directions with RUL are consistent. As RUL decreases, the features all change in the direction of increasing PC1. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 9(b), (d), and (e), the distributions of the principal components of their features differ significantly compared to Case 3, which is the reason for the lower accuracy of Cases 2, 4, and 5. Therefore, fine-tuning the model parameters using a limited number of training batteries in the target domain to enable it to output similar feature distributions as the batteries in the source domain is the key to model transfer in different domains.

To further validate the performance of the proposed life prediction model, additional validation on Dataset-NCA is conducted. As shown in Table 1, this section conducted model training and validation for NCA batteries, using charging segments for feature extraction, which differs from the discharging segments used in Test Task 3 and 4. Through the model validation in this section, it is demonstrated that the proposed model can generalize across different battery materials and is applicable to both charging and discharging features, which further expands the application scope of the proposed model.

Test Task 5. As shown in Fig. 10 (a)-(f), CTK, RUL, QRk, and QRe exhibit high prediction accuracy. Specifically, the prediction MAE for CTK and RUL is mainly within 20 cycles, while the prediction MAE for QRk and QRe is mainly 0.02. Furthermore, to further validate the performance of proposed model, the study conducted ten random splits of the dataset, followed by separate training and testing. As shown in Fig. 10(g) and (h), the proposed model demonstrates reliable performance on NCA batteries. This is evident from the ten repeated training and testing results, which show consistent accuracy. The average prediction MAPEs for CTK and RUL are 17.4% and 11.3%, respectively, while the average prediction MAPEs for QRk and QRe are 0.8% and 0.36%, respectively. The slightly poorer prediction accuracy of CTK may be due to the fact that the knee point of NCA battery itself is not as obvious as that of LFP battery, resulting in certain unavoidable errors when determining its knee point. In addition, since the knee point of the NCA battery is not significant, a slightly larger prediction error does not seriously affect the result of the trajectory reconstruction, as will be discussed later.

As illustrated in Fig. 11 , the fitting accuracy of the proposed three-parameter empirical model for capturing the degradation trajectory of NCA batteries is validated. Reconstructed trajectories originating from the 5th, 50th, and 100th cycles are presented for comparison. The results indicate that, in some cases, reconstructions initiated from the 5th cycle exhibit slightly lower accuracy. This can be attributed to the presence of an early turning point in the initial phase of NCA battery degradation. Nevertheless, the reconstructed curves still capture the overall degradation trend effectively. As the degradation progresses, the model's fitting accuracy improves significantly. Trajectories reconstructed from the 50th and 100th cycles show excellent agreement with the measured data. This outcome is expected, as the aging information embedded in a five-cycle segment becomes more pronounced with continued battery degradation. Overall, the proposed model demonstrates strong generalization capability across NCA battery degradation scenarios.

This study proposes a battery degradation staging model and a life prediction model, which enables the discrimination of battery degradation stages, the prediction of RUL and CTK, and puts forward a three-parameter empirical model to reconstruct battery capacity degradation trajectories. The main conclusions of this study are as follows.
(1)
The proposed battery degradation staging model can diagnose two degradation stages separated by knee points based on data from one cycle. The accuracy of the proposed model reaches 0.977,6 and 0.967,3 for LFP and NCA batteries, respectively. In addition, feature extraction is performed for LFP and NCA batteries under constant current discharging and constant current charging conditions, respectively. The proposed model demonstrates excellent performance across different battery types and feature conditions.
(2)
The proposed battery early life prediction model utilizes CNN, GAT, and LSTM to extract battery life features from only five cycles of data, enabling joint prediction of the battery's RUL and CTK. The high accuracy of the model is verified using LFP battery data, with prediction MAPEs of 10.5% and 12.8% for RUL and CTK, respectively. For NCA batteries, the model continues to perform excellently, with prediction MAPEs of 11.3% and 17.4% for RUL and CTK.
(3)
The coordinates of the starting point, knee point, and EOL point are quickly determined through the battery life prediction model's prediction results. Subsequently, a three-parameter empirical model is proposed to achieve rapid reconstruction of the battery's capacity degradation trajectory. The similarity between the reconstructed and actual capacity trajectories is as high as 0.98.
(4)
For the battery early life prediction model, a transfer learning strategy is proposed. By fine-tuning the model parameters using only three target domain battery data, accurate predictions for target domain batteries can be achieved. The prediction performance can match the accuracy of a model retrained with about 1:1 split of the target domain battery data.

The proposed framework is of great significance for rapid battery design assessment and retirement decisions. However, it is still difficult to predict the trajectory under uncertain working conditions in the future. Solving the limitation will be the focus of future work.

Can Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Renjie Wang: Validation, Investigation. Jianming Li: Methodology. Zhuangzhuang Li: Writing – original draft. Quanqing Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52177210 and U24A20158).

ce:section.ce:para: The data supporting the results of this study are from open-source datasets. The addresses of the datasets have been cited in the main text.
